narration.default: Every variable you make in C lives somewhere in the computer's memory. Each "somewhere" has a unique address — like a house number on a street. A POINTER is a special kind of variable that holds an address instead of a value. Think of it as an arrow that points AT another variable's box. Pointers sound scary at first, but they unlock two superpowers: a function can CHANGE a variable from outside (like scanf does with &age — yes, you've been using pointers without realizing!), and your program can work with arrays and strings efficiently. Today: meet pointers, learn the two operators (& for "address of" and * for "value at"), and use a function that changes a number from the outside.

steps.title: Every variable has a HOUSE ADDRESS

narration.default: Look at lines 8 and 10. Line 8: int x = 10; — a regular int variable. Line 10: printf("x lives at %p\n", (void*)&x); — the &x is "the ADDRESS of x". %p is the format code for printing addresses (p for pointer). The (void*) cast just tells printf "this address is not tied to any specific type, just print it". When you Run, you'll see something like 0x7ffe... — that's a hexadecimal number, the actual location of x in memory. The exact value changes each run; that's normal. The point is: every variable has one.

Line 9: int *p = &x; — let's break this down piece by piece. (1) int * means "this is a pointer to an int" — so p will hold the address of an int variable. The asterisk * goes BETWEEN the type and the name (some books put it next to the name like int *p, others next to the type like int* p — same thing). (2) p is the name. (3) = &x assigns the address of x to p. So now p HOLDS the address of x. p is itself a variable (it has its own address!) but the value INSIDE p is x's address. The arrow analogy: p is an arrow pointing to x.

steps.title: p points to the same address

narration.default: Line 11: printf("p points to %p\n", (void*)p); — note no & this time. We just print p directly because p ALREADY holds an address. When you Run, this address will MATCH the one from line 10 — &x and p give the same number, because p was set to &x. They are two ways to talk about the same memory location.

Line 12: printf("Value at p: %d\n", *p); — the *p in front of the variable is DEREFERENCING. It says "follow the arrow and grab the value at the other end". So *p is the same as x — both equal 10. Confusing fact: the * means TWO different things in C. In a declaration (int *p), the * says "this is a pointer". In an expression (*p), the * says "follow the pointer". Same symbol, different jobs depending on context. Read it carefully: int *p creates a pointer; *p uses a pointer.

Lines 3-5 define doubleIt: void doubleIt(int *p) { *p = *p * 2; }. The parameter is int *p — the function takes a POINTER, not an int. Inside, *p = *p * 2 means: read the value at p, multiply by 2, store the result back at p. So if p is pointing at x, this changes x itself — not a local copy. Without pointers, the function would only get a COPY of x and any change would vanish when the function returned. Pointers are how you give a function the power to modify its caller's variables.

Line 13: doubleIt(&x); — pass the address of x. Inside doubleIt, p becomes &x (the address). The function dereferences and writes — so x in main becomes 20. Notice scanf("%d", &age) works the same way: scanf takes a pointer, dereferences it, and writes the kid's number into your variable. Now you know WHY scanf needed the & — it needed a pointer to MODIFY the variable.

steps.title: Run — see addresses + the doubled x

narration.default: Click Run. The Console shows four lines. (1) "x lives at 0x..." — some hex address. (2) "p points to 0x..." — the SAME hex address (p was set to &x). (3) "Value at p: 10" — *p dereferences and reads x's value. (4) "After doubleIt: 20" — x got doubled by the function call. The exact hex addresses change each run; the relationships don't. p == &x, *p == x, and a function with a pointer parameter can change the original.
